content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

December 16, 1994

Ernie Tracy
P.O. Box 787
Dorris, California 96023-0787

Re: Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No. I-94-373

Dear Mr. Tracy:

We write in response to your request for advice regarding
your responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Since you have not named
specific decisions involved, we are treating this as a request for
informal issuance.² Your request, in part, raises issues under
the "Brown Act", California Government Code Sections 54950 et seq.
This agency is jurisdictionally limited to providing advice under
the Act, accordingly we are unable to address your questions
relating to other public laws which regulate the conduct of public
officials. We suggest you consult with your school board's
counsel or the county counsel's office for advice on these other
areas of public law.

QUESTION

Does your wife's employment with the Butte Valley Unified
School District (the "District") or your employment with the
Department of Food and Agriculture create a conflict of interest
for you as a member of the District's Governing Board?

_______________

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. I-94-373
Page 2


CONCLUSION

You may generally participate in decisions of the governing
board unless it is reasonably foreseeable that such decisions will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect
on the public generally, on you or your economic interests, as
defined below. Generally, a salary received from a government
entity does not give rise to an economic interest as defined in
the Act. Accordingly, neither your wife's employment with the
school district, nor your employment with the Department of Food
and Agriculture would, by itself, cause a conflict. However, you
may not participate in any decision to hire, fire, promote,
demote, or discipline your spouse, nor may you participate in a
decision to pay your spouse a salary which is different from
salaries paid to other employees of the agency in the same job
classification or position.

FACTS

You have been elected as a board member of the District
Governing Board. Your wife is employed by the Butte Valley
Unified School District as a secretary at the high school. She is
also vice-president of the classified employees' union.

You are a California State employee with the Department of
Food and Agriculture and a labor representative (a job steward)
for the California State Employees' Association.

ANALYSIS

The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prohibit a
public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
attempting to influence a governmental decision in which the
official knows or has reason to know that he or she has a
financial interest. (Sections 87100.) An official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect, on, among
other things, the official or any member of his or her immediate
family or, a source of income to the official of $250 or more
within the twelve months preceding the decision. (Section
87103.)3

Under Section 82030, salary, reimbursement for expenses and
per diem from a state, local, or federal government agency are
excluded from the definition of income. Accordingly, you can not
have a conflict of interest arising solely out of your salary as a
government employee or your community property interest in your
spouse's salary from the District. However, please note that a
decision to hire, fire, promote, demote, discipline, or pay your

3    The effect must be distinguishable from the decision's effect
on the public generally in order for the official to be
disqualified from participating in the decision.

File No. I-94-373
Page 3


spouse a salary which is different from the salary paid to other
employees in the same job classification, would be a decision in
which you would still be disqualified, as such a decision directly
affects your spouse, rather than merely affecting the source of
income. (Regulation 18702.1(c)(1).)

It is not apparent from your facts whether or not the
respective labor organizations to which you and your wife belong
are a source of income to you. If either or both labor
organizations are a source of income of $250 or more to you,⁴ then
you are potentially disqualified you from any decision which would
materially affect that source of income. Of course, it is not
possible to analyze whether or not a conflict would actually exist
with regard to these labor organizations without knowing the
nature of the decision involved. Should you require additional
advice with respect to a specific decision, we ask that you submit
a new request along with a complete recitation of all relevant
facts.

You have stated that your spouse may apply for a position as
softball coach. As stated above, you would have a conflict in any
decision to hire your spouse. Accordingly, you may not
participate in, or otherwise attempt to influence the decision to
hire a softball coach if your wife is an applicant for that
position. Attempts to influence include, but are not limited, to
official contacts with the decisionmaking body. (Regulation
18700.1)⁵

Should you have any further questions, you may contact the
undersigned at (916) 322-5901.


Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
[signature]
By: Daniel E. Muallem
Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosure

_______________
⁴ By virtue of the community property laws governing marital
property, any source of income of $500 or more to your spouse,
would be deemed to be a source of income of $250 or more to you.

⁵ A copy of Regulation 18700.1 is included for your reference.
